Evidence in page: 
Ildar Ibragimov (Tatar: Илдар Ибраһимов; Russian: Ильдар Ибрагимов; born 16 August 1967) is a Russian chess player. He was ranked No. 72 in January 2000 with a rating of 2611, while his peak rating of 2637 was achieved in April 2006. Ibragimov shared first place with Vladimir Kramnik and Andrei Kharlov at the 1991 USSR Under-26 Championship. He was awarded the title of Grandmaster by FIDE in 1993. Ibragimov won the Chigorin Memorial in 1994 and the Biel Masters Open tournament in 1997. In 2002 he started to play for the United States, where he lived in New Haven, Connecticut. He was a co-winner of the 2004 U.S. Open and shared third place in the 2006 U.S. Chess Championship. Ibragimov played for the US team that took the bronze medal in the 2006 Chess Olympiad. He tied for first in the 2006 World Open. In 2015 Ibragimov transferred his national federation back to Russia. He was part of the Russian team that won the gold medal at the European Senior Team Championship 2019 in the 50+ category.

Summary: 
The provided Wikipedia page for Ildar Ibragimov does not contain any information about his students. The content focuses entirely on his personal background, chess career, tournament achievements, and federation transfers.
</tool_response>

Evidence in page: 
Ildar Abdulovich Ibragimov (Ильдар Абдулович Ибрагимов, born 15 July 1932, Leningrad) is a Russian mathematician, specializing in probability theory and mathematical statistics.

Ibragimov is the son of a father who was an engineer with Bashkir ancestry and a mother who was a physician from a Tatar family with origins in Kazan. Ildar Ibragimov studied at Leningrad State University, where he graduated in mathematics in 1956. He received in 1960 his Russian candidate degree (Ph.D.) under Yuri Linnik and in 1967 his Russian doctorate (higher doctoral degree). In 1969 he became a professor of probability at Leningrad State University.

He is a senior scientist and director of the laboratory of statistical methods at the Steklov Institute in Saint Petersburg, a position he has held there since 1972 as the successor to Yuri Linnik. Ibragimov was elected in 1990 a corresponding member and in 1997 a full member of the Russian Academy of Sciences. In 1970 he received the Lenin Prize. In 1989 he was the Wald Lecturer of the Institute of Mathematical Statistics. In 1966 in Moscow he was an Invited Speaker of the ICM.

His doctoral students include Taivo Arak and Boris Tsirelson.

Selected publications:
- with Linnik: Independent and stationary sequences of random variables, Groningen, Wolters-Noordhoff 1971
- with Y. A. Rozanov: Gaussian Random Processes, Springer Verlag 1978
- with R. Z. Hasminskii: Statistical estimation, asymptotic theory, Springer Verlag 1981
- as editor with A. Yu. Zaitzev: Probability theory and mathematical statistics, Gordon and Breach 1996
- as editor with N. Balakrishnan, V. B. Nevzorov: Asymptotic methods in probability and statistics with applications, Birkhäuser 2001

Summary: 
Ildar Abdulovich Ibragimov (born July 15, 1932, in Leningrad) is a renowned Russian mathematician specializing in probability theory and mathematical statistics. He studied and later became a professor at Leningrad State University. Since 1972, he has served as a senior scientist and director of the laboratory of statistical methods at the Steklov Institute in Saint Petersburg. Throughout his distinguished career, he became a full member of the Russian Academy of Sciences, received the Lenin Prize in 1970, and was an Invited Speaker at the ICM in 1966. He has co-authored and edited numerous influential publications and mentored doctoral students like Taivo Arak and Boris Tsirelson. This information fully satisfies the user's goal of searching for details about the mathematician.
</tool_response>

<tool_call>
{"name": "visit", "arguments": {"goal": "Check Boris Tsirelson's page", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boris_Tsirelson"}}
</tool_call>

<tool_response>
[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boris_Tsirelson for user goal 'Check Boris Tsirelson's page' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Boris Semyonovich Tsirelson (Hebrew: בוריס סמיונוביץ' צירלסון, Russian: Борис Семёнович Цирельсон; May 4, 1950 – January 21, 2020) was a Russian–Israeli mathematician and Professor of Mathematics at Tel Aviv University in Israel, as well as a Wikipedia editor.
Tsirelson was born in Leningrad to a Russian Jewish family. From his father Simeon's side, he was the great-nephew of rabbi Yehuda Leib Tsirelson, chief rabbi of Bessarabia from 1918 to 1941, and a prominent posek and Jewish leader. He obtained his Master of Science from the University of Leningrad and remained there to pursue graduate studies. He obtained his Ph.D. in 1975, with thesis "General properties of bounded Gaussian processes and related questions" written under the direction of Ildar Abdulovich Ibragimov.
Later, he participated in the refusenik movement, but only received permission to immigrate to Israel in 1991. From then until 2017, he was a professor at Tel-Aviv University.
Tsirelson made notable contributions to probability theory and functional analysis. These include:
Tsirelson's bound, in quantum mechanics, is an inequality, related to the issue of quantum nonlocality.
Tsirelson space is an example of a reflexive Banach space in which neither a l p space nor a c0 space can be embedded.
The Tsirelson's drift, a counterexample in the theory of stochastic differential equations, is an SDE which has a weak solution but no strong solution.
The Gaussian isoperimetric inequality (proved by Vladimir Sudakov and Tsirelson, and independently by Christer Borell), stating that affine halfspaces are the isoperimetric sets for the Gaussian measure.
Tsirelson died on January 21, 2020, at the age of 69.

Summary: 
Boris Tsirelson (May 4, 1950 – January 21, 2020) was a Russian-Israeli mathematician, a professor at Tel Aviv University, and a Wikipedia editor. Born in Leningrad to a Russian Jewish family, he earned his Ph.D. in 1975 under Ildar Ibragimov. Active in the refusenik movement, he moved to Israel in 1991. Tsirelson is renowned for his significant contributions to functional analysis and probability theory, including Tsirelson's bound, Tsirelson space, Tsirelson's drift, and the Gaussian isoperimetric inequality. This information provides a comprehensive overview of his life and achievements, fulfilling the user's request.
</tool_response>

Evidence in page: 
Taivo Arak (2 November 1946, Tallinn – 17 October 2007, Stockholm) was an Estonian mathematician, specializing in probability theory.

In 1969 he graduated from Leningrad State University. There he received in 1972 his Russian candidate degree (Ph.D.) under I. A. Ibragimov. In 1983 Arak defended his dissertation for his Russian doctorate (higher doctoral degree similar to habilitation).

From 1972 to 1981 he worked at the Tallinn University of Technology. From 1981 he worked at the Institute of Cybernetics of the Academy of Sciences of the Estonian SSR. In 1986 he was an Invited Speaker at the International Congress of Mathematicians in Berkeley, California. Most of his research dealt with the theory of probability.

Awards: Markov Prize (1983) - for the series of papers 'Равномерные предельные теоремы для сумм независимых случайных величин' (Uniform limit theorems for sums of independent random variables).

Summary: 
Taivo Arak (1946–2007) was an Estonian mathematician who specialized in probability theory. He graduated from Leningrad State University in 1969, where he later earned his Ph.D. in 1972 and his Russian doctorate in 1983. Throughout his career, he worked at the Tallinn University of Technology and the Institute of Cybernetics of the Academy of Sciences of the Estonian SSR. He was an invited speaker at the 1986 International Congress of Mathematicians and won the Markov Prize in 1983 for his work on uniform limit theorems. This fully satisfies the goal of checking and summarizing Taivo Arak's Wikipedia page.
</tool_response>

[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ofer_Zeitouni for user goal 'Check Ofer Zeitouni's page' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Ofer Zeitouni (Hebrew: עפר זיתוני; born 23 October 1960, Haifa) is an Israeli mathematician, specializing in probability theory.

Zeitouni received his bachelor's degree in electrical engineering in 1980 from the Technion. He obtained in 1986 his doctorate in electrical engineering under the supervision of Moshe Zakai with the thesis Bounds on the Conditional Density and Maximum a posteriori Estimators for the Nonlinear Filtering Problem. As a postdoc he was a visiting assistant professor at Brown University and at the Laboratory for Information and Decision Systems at MIT. He joined the Technion in 1989 as senior lecturer, and was promoted in 1991 to associate professor, and in 1997 to full professor in the department of electrical engineering. He is now a professor of Mathematics at the Weizmann Institute and at the Courant Institute, and was from 2002 to 2013 a part-time professor at the University of Minnesota.

His research deals with stochastic processes and filtering theory with applications to control theory (electrical engineering), the spectral theory of random matrices, the theory of large deviations in probability theory, motion in random media, and extremes of logarithmically correlated fields.

He was Invited Speaker with the talk Random Walks in Random Environments at the ICM in Beijing in 2002. Zeitouni is a Fellow of the American Mathematical Society, member of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, the National Academy of Sciences, and the Israel Academy of Sciences and Humanities.

Summary: 
Ofer Zeitouni is an Israeli mathematician specializing in probability theory, born on October 23, 1960, in Haifa. He earned his bachelor's and doctoral degrees in electrical engineering from the Technion, the latter under the supervision of Moshe Zakai. After postdoctoral work at Brown University and MIT, he became a professor at the Technion. He is currently a mathematics professor at the Weizmann Institute of Science and the Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences. His research focuses on stochastic processes, filtering theory, random matrices, and large deviations. He is a fellow of several prestigious academic societies, including the American Mathematical Society and the National Academy of Sciences.
</tool_response>

<tool_response>
[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nail_Bakirov for user goal 'Check Nail Bakirov's page' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Nail Kutluzhanovich Bakirov (Russian: Наиль Кутлужанович Бакиров, Tatar: Наил Котлыҗан улы Бәкирев, romanized: Nail Kotlıcan uğlı Bäkirev) (30 March 1952 – 23 March 2010) was a prominent Russian statistician, professor and member of the Russian Academy of Sciences in Ufa, Russia, known for his work in the asymptotic theory of mathematical statistics.

Bakirov was born in the city of Karaganda, Kazakhstan. His father had a doctorate in geology and worked for an oil company and his mother was an ophthalmologist. In 1969 he graduated from high school in his native Kazakhstan and was accepted at the Department of Mathematics and Mechanics at Moscow State University. He earned a doctoral degree from the St. Petersburg State University in 1979. Since 1974 his life was connected with Ufa. When the Ufa Mathematics Institute was founded in 1988, Bakirov became its member. Most of his research was devoted to probability theory and mathematical statistics.

In 2010 he died in a road accident (hit by a car) on the way home from work in Ufa, Russia.

Summary: 
Nail Kutluzhanovich Bakirov (1952–2010) was a prominent Russian statistician, professor, and member of the Russian Academy of Sciences. Born in Karaganda, Kazakhstan, he studied at Moscow State University and received his doctorate from St. Petersburg State University in 1979. He spent most of his career in Ufa, Russia, focusing his research on probability theory and mathematical statistics. He died in a road accident in 2010. This information provides a comprehensive overview of his life and career as requested.
</tool_response>

Estate V. Khmaladze (Georgian: ესტატე ხმალაძე, born October 20, 1944, in Tbilisi, Georgia) is a Georgian statistician. He is best known for his contribution of Khmaladze transformation in statistics.

Biography
Estate Khmaladze was born October 20, 1944, Tbilisi, Georgia. He graduated from Tbilisi State University in 1966, where the first three years he was studying physics. He finished his PhD in 1971 at V. A. Steklov Mathematical Institute, Moscow, under supervision of L. N. Bolshev, who was head of department of mathematical statistics at Steklov after N. V. Smirnov. From 1972 until 1990, his work was, mostly, split between the Steklov Institute of Mathematics in Moscow, and the A. Razmadze Mathematical Institute at Tbilisi State University.

From 1990 to 1999, he was appointed head of department of probability theory and mathematical statistics of A. Razmadze Mathematical Institute of the Georgian National Academy of Sciences.

In 1996, Khmaladze moved with his family from Tbilisi, Georgia, to Sydney, Australia, and from there to Wellington, New Zealand, where in 2002 he was appointed Professor of Statistics after retirement of his predecessor, David Vere-Jones.

A characteristic feature of Khmaladze's work is the search of connection between distant analytical topics. For example, in Khmaladze (1993), the connections between the theory of spatial martingales and Volterra operators with goodness of fit problems of statistics was demonstrated, and in Khmaladze(2007), the infinitesimal theory for set-valued functions was extended to help with problems of spatial statistics and image analysis.

However, the majority of his mathematical research centers around empirical processes and distribution-free methods of testing statistical hypotheses.

A considerable amount of Khmaladze's work is in applications of statistics, in the fields of cito-genetics, physiology, demography and insurance, statistical analysis of texts, various problems in economics and finance. His current applied interests are focused on statistical theory of diversity and Zipf's law.

Summary: 
Estate V. Khmaladze (born October 20, 1944, in Tbilisi, Georgia) is a prominent Georgian-Australian statistician and mathematician best known for the Khmaladze transformation. He studied physics and graduated from Tbilisi State University in 1966, later completing his PhD in 1971 at the V. A. Steklov Mathematical Institute in Moscow under L. N. Bolshev. Over his career, he worked at both the Steklov Institute and the A. Razmadze Mathematical Institute, heading the latter's department of probability theory and mathematical statistics from 1990 to 1999. In 1996, he moved to Oceania, eventually becoming a Professor of Statistics at the Victoria University of Wellington in New Zealand in 2002. His research frequently connects distant analytical topics, though it primarily focuses on empirical processes and distribution-free hypothesis testing methods, with various applications extending to fields like demography, finance, and Zipf's law.
</tool_response>

Evidence in page: 
The St. Petersburg Department of Steklov Institute of Mathematics of the Russian Academy of Sciences (Russian: Санкт-Петербургское отделение Математического института им. В. А. Стеклова РАН, abbreviated ПОМИ (PDMI) for "Петербургское отделение Математического института", Petersburg Department of the Mathematical Institute; PDMI) is a mathematical research institute in St. Petersburg, part of the Russian Academy of Sciences. Until 1992 it was known as Leningrad Department of Steklov Institute of Mathematics of the USSR Academy of Sciences (ЛОМИ, LOMI). The name of the institution is a historical tradition and since 1995 it has no subordination to the Steklov Institute of Mathematics. The institute was established in 1940 as a department of the Steklov Institute and is named after Vladimir Andreevich Steklov, a Soviet/Russian mathematician, mechanician and physicist. Directors: V. A. Tartakovskii (1940–1941), Andrey Markov, Jr. (1942–1953), Nikolai Erugin (1953–1957), Georgii Petrashen' (1957–1976), Ludvig Faddeev (1976–2000), Ildar Abdulovich Ibragimov (2000–2006), Sergei Kislyakov (2007–2021), Maxim Vsemirnov (2021–present). Notable researchers: Aleksandr Danilovich Aleksandrov, Yuri Burago, Nikolai Durov, Dmitry Konstantinovich Faddeev, Vera Faddeeva, Fedor Fomin, Leonid Kantorovich, Vladimir Korepin, Olga Ladyzhenskaya, Yuri Linnik, Yuri Matiyasevich, Grigori Perelman worked at this institution when he proved the Poincaré conjecture, Fedor Petrov, Nicolai Reshetikhin, Nikolai Aleksandrovich Shanin, Samson Shatashvili, Andrei Suslin, Leon Takhtajan, Anatoly Vershik, Alexander Volberg, Oleg Viro, Victor Zalgaller.

Summary: 
The St. Petersburg Department of Steklov Institute of Mathematics of the Russian Academy of Sciences is a prominent mathematical research institute established in 1940. Originally a department of the Steklov Institute, it became independent in 1995. The page lists its directors from 1940 to the present, including current director Maxim Vsemirnov, and highlights numerous notable researchers who worked there, such as Grigori Perelman, who proved the Poincaré conjecture during his time at the institute. This information successfully fulfills the user's goal of checking the details of the St. Petersburg Department of Steklov Mathematical Institute page.
</tool_response>

Evidence in page: 
Aleksandr Alekseevich Borovkov (Russian: Александр Алексеевич Боровков; 6 March 1931 – 15 May 2026) was a Russian mathematician. Borovkov was born in Moscow on 6 March 1931. He received his Russian candidate degree (Ph.D.) in 1959 under Andrey Kolmogorov at Moscow State University and his Russian doctorate (higher doctoral degree) in 1963. He was an academician at the Sobolev Institute of Mathematics of the Siberian Branch of the Russian Academy of Sciences and a professor at the Novosibirsk State University. His research dealt with probability theory, mathematical statistics, and stochastic processes. He was an Invited Speaker of the ICM in 1966 in Moscow and in 1978 in Helsinki (Rate of convergence and large deviations in invariance principle). Borovkov was elected in 1966 a corresponding member and in 1990 a full member of the Russian Academy of Sciences. In 1979 he received the USSR State Prize. He was also winner of the Kolmogorov Prize in 2015. Borovkov died on 15 May 2026, at the age of 95. Selected publications: Stochastic processes in queueing theory. Springer, 1976; Asymptotic methods in queuing theory. Wiley, 1984; Probability Theory. New York: Gordon & Breach, 1998; Mathematical Statistics. New York: Gordon & Breach, 1998; Ergodicity and stability of stochastic processes. New York: Wiley, 1998; with A. A. Mogulskii: Large deviations and testing statistical hypothesis, Siber. Adv. Math., 1992, 1993; with Konstantin A. Borovkov: Asymptotic analysis of random walks. Series: Encyclopedia of Mathematics and Its Applications, Vol. 118. Cambridge University Press, 2008.

Summary: 
Aleksandr Alekseevich Borovkov (March 6, 1931 – May 15, 2026) was a prominent Russian mathematician known for his work in probability theory, mathematical statistics, and stochastic processes. He earned his Ph.D. under Andrey Kolmogorov at Moscow State University in 1959. Throughout his career, he served as an academician at the Sobolev Institute of Mathematics and a professor at Novosibirsk State University. His significant contributions were recognized with elections to the Russian Academy of Sciences, the USSR State Prize in 1979, and the Kolmogorov Prize in 2015. He authored several notable publications on stochastic processes and queueing theory.
</tool_response>

<tool_call>
{"name": "visit", "arguments": {"goal": "Check Taivo Arak's page", "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taivo_Arak"}}
</tool_call>

<tool_response>
[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taivo_Arak for user goal 'Check Taivo Arak's page' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Taivo Arak (2 November 1946, Tallinn – 17 October 2007, Stockholm) was an Estonian mathematician, specializing in probability theory.

In 1969 he graduated from Leningrad State University. [1] There he received in 1972 his Russian candidate degree (Ph.D.) under I. A. Ibragimov. [2]

In 1983 Arak defended his dissertation for his Russian doctorate (higher doctoral degree similar to habilitation).

From 1972 to 1981 he worked at the Tallinn University of Technology. From 1981 he worked at the Institute of Cybernetics of the Academy of Sciences of the Estonian SSR. [3] In 1986 he was an Invited Speaker at the International Congress of Mathematicians in Berkeley, California. [4]

Most of his research dealt with the theory of probability.

Awards
Markov Prize (1983) - for the series of papers "Равномерные предельные теоремы для сумм независимых случайных величин" (Uniform limit theorems for sums of independent random variables).

Selected publications
with Andrei Yuryevich Zaitsev: Uniform limit theorems for sums of independent random variables. Proceedings of the Steklov Institute of Mathematics, Vol. 174. American Mathematical Soc. 1988.
with Donatas Surgailis: Arak, T.; Surgailis, D. (February 1989). "Markov fields with polygonal realizations". Probability Theory and Related Fields. 80 (4): 543–579.
with D. Surgailis: Grigelionis, Bronius (1990). "Markov random graphs and polygonal fields with Y-shaped nodes". Probability theory and mathematical statistics. Proc. 5th Vilnius conference, vol. 1. pp. 57–67.
with Peter Clifford and D. Surgailis: Arak, T.; Clifford, P.; Surgailis, D. (1993). "Point-based polygonal models for random graphs". Advances in Applied Probability. 25 (2): 348–372.

Summary: 
Taivo Arak (1946–2007) was an Estonian mathematician who specialized in probability theory. He earned his degrees from Leningrad State University, working under I. A. Ibragimov. Professionally, Arak served at the Tallinn University of Technology and the Institute of Cybernetics of the Academy of Sciences of the Estonian SSR. He was an Invited Speaker at the 1986 International Congress of Mathematicians and won the Markov Prize in 1983 for his work on limit theorems. The page also lists several of his collaborative publications. This fully answers the user's request for information on Taivo Arak.
</tool_response>

[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ernesto_Mordecki for user goal 'Check Ernesto Mordecki page.' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Ernesto Mordecki Pupko 6 October 1962 (age 63) Montevideo, Uruguay
Occupations mathematician, professor
Ernesto Mordecki Pupko (born 6 October 1962) is a Uruguayan mathematician and professor.
Mordecki is Professor at the Centro de Matemática of the University of the Republic, Uruguay. He received his Ph.D. in statistics of stochastic processes in 1994 from the Steklov Mathematical Institute, under the supervision of Albert Shiryaev. His research interests include optimal stopping of stochastic processes and applications to finance. During 2000–2001 Mordecki was the director of the Centro de Matemática (Mathematics Center), Science Faculty, Montevideo.
Mordecki has authored more than 40 articles on his research interests.
In 2015 he was elected member of the Academy of Sciences of Uruguay

Summary: 
Ernesto Mordecki (born October 6, 1962, in Montevideo) is a Uruguayan mathematician and professor. He currently teaches at the Centro de Matemática of the University of the Republic, Uruguay, where he also served as director from 2000 to 2001. He earned his Ph.D. in statistics of stochastic processes in 1994 from the Steklov Mathematical Institute under Albert Shiryaev. His research, which has led to over 40 published articles, focuses on the optimal stopping of stochastic processes and their applications in finance. Additionally, he was elected to the Academy of Sciences of Uruguay in 2015.
</tool_response>

Nikolai Vasilyevich Smirnov
Born 17 October 1900 Moscow, Russian Empire
Died 2 June 1966 (aged 65) Moscow, Soviet Union
Alma mater Moscow State University
Known for Kolmogorov–Smirnov test
Awards State Prize of the U.S.S.R., Order of the Red Banner of Labor
Fields Mathematics
Institutions Steklov Institute of Mathematics

Nikolai Vasilyevich Smirnov (Russian: Николай Васильевич Смирнов; 17 October 1900 – 2 June 1966) was a Soviet Russian mathematician noted for his work in various fields including probability theory and statistics. Smirnov's principal works in mathematical statistics and probability theory were devoted to the investigation of limit distributions by means of the asymptotic behaviour of multiple integrals as the multiplicity is increased with limit. He was one of the creators of the nonparametric methods in mathematical statistics and of the theory of limit distributions of order statistics.

Together with Andrey Kolmogorov, Smirnov developed the Kolmogorov–Smirnov test and participated in the creation of the Cramér–von Mises–Smirnov criterion. Smirnov made great efforts to popularize and widely disseminate methods of mathematical statistics in the natural sciences and engineering.

Summary: 
Nikolai Vasilyevich Smirnov (1900–1966) was a distinguished Soviet Russian mathematician renowned for his foundational work in probability theory and statistics. Educated at Moscow State University, he spent much of his career at the Steklov Institute of Mathematics. He is most famously known for co-developing the Kolmogorov–Smirnov test with Andrey Kolmogorov and for his pioneering role in creating nonparametric methods in mathematical statistics. Over his career, he made significant efforts to apply statistical methods in engineering and the natural sciences, earning him accolades such as the Stalin Prize and the Order of the Red Banner of Labor.
</tool_response>

<tool_response>
[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boris_Tsirelson for user goal 'Check Boris Tsirelson page' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Boris Semyonovich Tsirelson (May 4, 1950 – January 21, 2020) was a Russian–Israeli mathematician and Professor of Mathematics at Tel Aviv University in Israel, as well as a Wikipedia editor.

Born May 4, 1950 Leningrad, Soviet Union
Died January 21, 2020 (aged 69) Basel, Switzerland
Known for Tsirelson's bound, Tsirelson space, Tsirelson's stochastic differential equation, Gaussian isoperimetric inequality
Thesis: General properties of bounded Gaussian processes and related questions (1975)
Doctoral advisor: Ildar Ibragimov

Tsirelson was born in Leningrad to a Russian Jewish family... He obtained his Master of Science from the University of Leningrad and remained there to pursue graduate studies. He obtained his Ph.D. in 1975... Later, he participated in the refusenik movement, but only received permission to immigrate to Israel in 1991. From then until 2017, he was a professor at Tel-Aviv University.

Tsirelson made notable contributions to probability theory and functional analysis. These include: Tsirelson's bound, in quantum mechanics... Tsirelson space is an example of a reflexive Banach space... The Tsirelson's drift, a counterexample in the theory of stochastic differential equations... The Gaussian isoperimetric inequality

Summary: 
Boris Semyonovich Tsirelson (1950–2020) was a Russian-Israeli mathematician and Professor of Mathematics at Tel Aviv University. Born in Leningrad, he received his Ph.D. in 1975 before immigrating to Israel in 1991. He is highly regarded for his significant contributions to probability theory and functional analysis, most notably Tsirelson's bound in quantum mechanics, Tsirelson space, Tsirelson's stochastic differential equation, and the Gaussian isoperimetric inequality. He was also known as an active Wikipedia editor.
</tool_response>

Boris Semyonovich Tsirelson (1950–2020) was a Russian-Israeli mathematician and Professor of Mathematics at Tel Aviv University. Born in Leningrad, he received his Ph.D. in 1975 before immigrating to Israel in 1991. He is highly regarded for his significant contributions to probability theory and functional analysis, most notably Tsirelson's bound in quantum mechanics, Tsirelson space, Tsirelson's stochastic differential equation, and the Gaussian isoperimetric inequality. He was also known as an active Wikipedia editor.

Taivo Arak (2 November 1946, Tallinn – 17 October 2007, Stockholm) was an Estonian mathematician, specializing in probability theory.
In 1969 he graduated from Leningrad State University. There he received in 1972 his Russian candidate degree (Ph.D.) under I. A. Ibragimov.
In 1983 Arak defended his dissertation for his Russian doctorate (higher doctoral degree similar to habilitation).
From 1972 to 1981 he worked at the Tallinn University of Technology. From 1981 he worked at the Institute of Cybernetics of the Academy of Sciences of the Estonian SSR. In 1986 he was an Invited Speaker at the International Congress of Mathematicians in Berkeley, California.
Most of his research dealt with the theory of probability.
Awards
Markov Prize (1983) - for the series of papers "Равномерные предельные теоремы для сумм независимых случайных величин" (Uniform limit theorems for sums of independent random variables).

Evidence in page: 
Romanas Januškevičius
Born 10 July 1953 Vilnius, Lithuania
Alma mater Vilnius University Steklov Institute
Known for Characterization of probability distributions and its stability
Awards Order of Holy Prince Daniel of Moscow (3rd degree)
Scientific career
Fields Mathematics
Doctoral advisor Vladimir Zolotarev
Romanas Januškevičius (sometimes transliterated from Russian as Romanas Yanushkevichius; born July 10, 1953) is a Lithuanian mathematician who worked in probability theory and characterization of probability distributions and its stability. He was a professor at the Lithuanian University of Educational Sciences, head of the Department of Mathematics, Informatics and Physics since 2001 until 2017.
Early life
Januškevičius was born in Vilnius on July 10, 1953. He graduated from one of Vilnius high schools in 1971 and entered Vilnius University, from which he graduated in 1976. In 1976–1978 Januškevičius trained in Steklov Institute of Mathematics in Moscow, where under the guidance of Professor Vladimir Zolotarev wrote and in 1978 defended his thesis "Investigation of stability in some problems of characterization of distributions" and received the Candidate of Sciences degree. The main result of this thesis was the work of the author, which considered the stability for decompositions of probability distributions into components.
Work and research interests
Januškevičius received the Doctor of Science degree (habilitation) in 1993. His doctoral thesis "Stability for characterizations of distributions" was published as a monograph... Januškevičius was a professor at the Vilnius Pedagogical University since 1997 until 2017. The main emphasis of his pedagogical activity is training of teachers of mathematics and informatics.
Awards
Patriarch Alexy II during his visit to Vilnius on 25–27 July 1997 personally awarded Januškevičius with the Order of Holy Prince Daniel of Moscow of III degree for the diligent work for the blessing of Orthodoxy in Lithuania in the affair of revival of spirituality.

Summary: 
Romanas Januškevičius (born July 10, 1953, in Vilnius, Lithuania) is a Lithuanian mathematician specializing in probability theory and the characterization of probability distributions and their stability. He earned his degrees from Vilnius University and the Steklov Institute of Mathematics, studying under Vladimir Zolotarev. He served as a professor at the Lithuanian University of Educational Sciences and headed the Department of Mathematics, Informatics, and Physics from 2001 to 2017. In addition to his mathematical work, he has co-authored a prominent book on ethics and was awarded the Order of Holy Prince Daniel of Moscow (3rd degree) by Patriarch Alexy II in 1997 for his contributions to spirituality and Orthodoxy in Lithuania.
</tool_response>

Evidence in page: 
Nikolai Georgievich Makarov (Russian: Николай Георгиевич Макаров; born January 1955) is a Russian-American mathematician. He is known for his work in complex analysis and its applications to dynamical systems, probability theory and mathematical physics. He is currently the Richard Merkin Distinguished Professor of Mathematics at Caltech, where he has been teaching since 1991.

Makarov belongs to the Leningrad school of geometric function theory. He graduated from the Leningrad State University with a bachelor's degree in 1982. He received his Ph.D. (Candidate of Science) from the Steklov Institute of Mathematics in 1986 under Nikolai Nikolski with thesis Metric properties of harmonic measure (title translated from Russian). He was an academic at the Steklov Institute of Mathematics in Leningrad. Since 1991 he has been a professor at Caltech.

In 1986 he was an Invited Speaker of the ICM in Berkeley, California. In 1986 he was awarded the Salem Prize for solving difficult problems involving the boundary behavior of the conformal mapping of a disk onto a domain with a Jordan curve boundary using stochastic methods. In 2020, he was awarded the Rolf Schock Prize, "for his significant contributions to complex analysis and its applications to mathematical physics".

Makarov works in complex analysis and related fields (potential theory, harmonic analysis, spectral theory) as well as on various applications to complex dynamics, random matrices and mathematical conformal field theory.

Summary: 
Nikolai Georgievich Makarov is a renowned Russian-American mathematician known for his contributions to complex analysis, dynamical systems, probability theory, and mathematical physics. [visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nikolai_Georgievich_Makarov for user goal 'Check Nikolai Makarov page' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Nikolai Georgievich Makarov (Russian: Николай Георгиевич Макаров; born January 1955) is a Russian-American mathematician. He is known for his work in complex analysis and its applications to dynamical systems, probability theory and mathematical physics. He is currently the Richard Merkin Distinguished Professor of Mathematics at Caltech, where he has been teaching since 1991.

Makarov belongs to the Leningrad school of geometric function theory. He graduated from the Leningrad State University with a bachelor's degree in 1982. He received his Ph.D. (Candidate of Science) from the Steklov Institute of Mathematics in 1986 under Nikolai Nikolski with thesis Metric properties of harmonic measure (title translated from Russian). He was an academic at the Steklov Institute of Mathematics in Leningrad. Since 1991 he has been a professor at Caltech.

In 1986 he was an Invited Speaker of the ICM in Berkeley, California. In 1986 he was awarded the Salem Prize for solving difficult problems involving the boundary behavior of the conformal mapping of a disk onto a domain with a Jordan curve boundary using stochastic methods. In 2020, he was awarded the Rolf Schock Prize, "for his significant contributions to complex analysis and its applications to mathematical physics".

Makarov works in complex analysis and related fields (potential theory, harmonic analysis, spectral theory) as well as on various applications to complex dynamics, random matrices and mathematical conformal field theory.

He earned his Ph.D. from the Steklov Institute of Mathematics in 1986 and has been serving as a professor at Caltech since 1991. Throughout his career, Makarov has received significant recognition, including the Salem Prize in 1986 and the Rolf Schock Prize in 2020. His research primarily focuses on complex analysis, complex dynamics, random matrices, and mathematical conformal field theory. messages.content:
You are a web navigation agent. Your task is to navigate a real website to complete a specific user task by interacting with the browser.

You are already on the starting page. Use the available tools to navigate the website step by step:
- Use `click` to click buttons, links, or filters
- Use `fill` to type into search boxes or input fields
- Use `visit` ONLY to refresh the current page or navigate to a URL shown in the page Evidence — NEVER construct or guess URLs yourself
- Do NOT use `search` to do a Google search — stay on the current website and navigate within it

## CRITICAL: No manual URL construction

- You MUST interact with the website through its UI (click, fill) to navigate. Do NOT construct search query URLs, append query parameters, or guess URL patterns.
- The `visit` tool should only be used with: (1) the starting URL, (2) URLs that appear in the page Evidence, or (3) the current page URL to refresh the snapshot.
- If you cannot figure out how to navigate via the UI, try different UI elements — do NOT fall back to URL construction.

## CRITICAL: One tool call at a time

- You MUST call only ONE tool per turn. Wait for the result and observe the updated Evidence before calling the next tool.
- NEVER call multiple `fill` or `click` tools in parallel — each interaction may change the page DOM and invalidate other refs.

## Interacting with combobox / autocomplete fields

- For airport, city, or any autocomplete/combobox input: first call `fill` to type the text, then in the returned Evidence, find and `click` the matching dropdown suggestion to confirm the selection.
- Do NOT move to the next field until the current combobox selection is confirmed via a `click` on the dropdown option.
- After clicking a dropdown option to confirm a combobox selection, the returned Evidence will show the updated page state. Do NOT call `visit` to refresh — proceed directly to the next form field using refs from the returned Evidence.

## How to submit your final answer

Navigate to the page that contains the answer, read the information from the page, then output the extracted text inside <answer></answer> tags:
<answer>The cheapest flight is UA837 at $1,240 total.</answer>

NEVER put a URL inside <answer> — always extract and output the specific value requested.

## Error recovery

- If `fill` or `click` returns "ref not found", call `visit` on the current page URL to refresh the page snapshot, then retry using a new ref from the updated Evidence.
- Do NOT retry the exact same ref after a "ref not found" error.
- If the same step fails more than 2 times, try a different interaction strategy on the same page (e.g., click a different element, use a different input format). You must always navigate through the website's UI.
- Always use the exact dates and year from the task — do not substitute a different year.

# Tools

You may call one or more functions to assist with the user task.

You are provided with function signatures within <tools></tools> XML tags:
<tools>
{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "visit", "description": "Visit the webpage and return a summary of its content.", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"url": {"type": "string", "description": "The URL of the webpage to visit."}, "goal": {"type": "string", "description": "The goal or intent of visiting the webpage, i.e., what information you want to extract from this page."}}, "required": ["url", "goal"]}}}
{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "click", "description": "Click an identified element based on its reference index and return a summary of the content after clicking. You are only allowed to click items that come from the latest visit/click tool's clickable results (they appear in the Evidence in page section).", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"ref": {"type": "string", "description": "The unique identifier for the element to be clicked on the current page. Must come from a notation like [ref=XXX] in the latest Evidence in page."}, "goal": {"type": "string", "description": "The goal or intent of performing this click, i.e., what information you want to obtain after clicking."}}, "required": ["ref", "goal"]}}}
{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "fill", "description": "Enter text content into an input field and return the filled state. You are only allowed to fill items that come from the latest visit/click tool's fillable results (they appear in the Evidence in page section).", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"ref": {"type": "string", "description": "The unique identifier for the element to be filled. Must come from a notation like [ref=XXX] in the latest Evidence in page."}, "text": {"type": "string", "description": "The content to be entered into the input field."}}, "required": ["ref", "text"]}}}
</tools>

For each function call, return a json object with function name and arguments within <tool_call></tool_call> XML tags:
<tool_call>
{"name": <function-name>, "arguments": <args-json-object>}
</tool_call>

- Use `visit` ONLY to refresh the current page or navigate to a URL shown in the page Evidence — NEVER construct or guess URLs yourself
- Do NOT use `search` to do a Google search — stay on the current website and navigate within it

## CRITICAL: No manual URL construction

- You MUST interact with the website through its UI (click, fill) to navigate. Do NOT construct search query URLs, append query parameters, or guess URL patterns.
- The `visit` tool should only be used with: (1) the starting URL, (2) URLs that appear in the page Evidence, or (3) the current page URL to refresh the snapshot.
- If you cannot figure out how to navigate via the UI, try different UI elements — do NOT fall back to URL construction.

## CRITICAL: One tool call at a time

- You MUST call only ONE tool per turn. Wait for the result and observe the updated Evidence before calling the next tool.
- NEVER call multiple `fill` or `click` tools in parallel — each interaction may change the page DOM and invalidate other refs.

## Interacting with combobox / autocomplete fields

- For airport, city, or any autocomplete/combobox input: first call `fill` to type the text, then in the returned Evidence, find and `click` the matching dropdown suggestion to confirm the selection.
- Do NOT move to the next field until the current combobox selection is confirmed via a `click` on the dropdown option.
- After clicking a dropdown option to confirm a combobox selection, the returned Evidence will show the updated page state. Do NOT call `visit` to refresh — proceed directly to the next form field using refs from the returned Evidence.

## How to submit your final answer

Navigate to the page that contains the answer, read the information from the page, then output the extracted text inside <answer></answer> tags:
<answer>The cheapest flight is UA837 at $1,240 total.</answer>

NEVER put a URL inside <answer> — always extract and output the specific value requested.

## Error recovery

- If `fill` or `click` returns "ref not found", call `visit` on the current page URL to refresh the page snapshot, then retry using a new ref from the updated Evidence.
- Do NOT retry the exact same ref after a "ref not found" error.
- If the same step fails more than 2 times, try a different interaction strategy on the same page (e.g., click a different element, use a different input format). You must always navigate through the website's UI.
- Always use the exact dates and year from the task — do not substitute a different year.

[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sergei_Viktorovich_Bochkarev for user goal 'check if Sergei Viktorovich Bochkarev matches the clues' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Sergei (or Sergey) Viktorovich Bochkarev (or Bočkarev) (Сергей Викторович Бочкарёв, born July 24, 1941, in Kuybyshev now renamed Samara) is a Soviet and Russian mathematician.

He received in 1964 his undergraduate degree from Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology and in 1969 his Russian Candidate of Sciences degree (PhD) from Moscow State University. His dissertation о рядах Фурье по системе Хаара [1] (On Fourier series in the Haar system) was supervised by Pyotr Lavrentyevich Ulyanov. [2] [3] From Moscow State University, Bochkarev received in 1974 his Russian Doctor of Science degree (habilitation). [4] Since 1971 he has worked at the Steklov Institute of Mathematics, where he holds the title of leading scientific researcher in the Department of Function Theory.

His research deals with harmonic analysis, BMO spaces, Hardy spaces, functional analysis, construction of orthogonal bases in various function spaces, and exponential sums.

In 1977 he was awarded the Salem Prize. In 1978 he was an Invited Speaker with talk Метод усреднения в теории ортогональных рядов (The averaging method in the theory of orthogonal bases) at the International Congress of Mathematicians in Helsinki.

Summary: 
Sergei Viktorovich Bochkarev (born July 24, 1941) is a notable Soviet and Russian mathematician specializing in harmonic analysis, BMO spaces, and functional analysis. Educated at the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology and Moscow State University, he has been a leading researcher at the Steklov Institute of Mathematics since 1971. His prominent achievements include receiving the Salem Prize in 1977 and speaking at the 1978 International Congress of Mathematicians. This comprehensive biographical and professional data provides the necessary details to cross-reference with any specific clues the user may have.
</tool_response>

Evidence in page: 
Boris Sergeevich Kashin (Russian: Борис Сергеевич Кашин; born July 3, 1951, in Moscow) is a Russian mathematician, Academician of the Russian Academy of Sciences (since 2011), Doctor of Sciences, Professor at the MSU Faculty of Mechanics and Mathematics. Member of the Central Committee of the Communist Party of the Russian Federation since 2000.
He graduated from the MSU Faculty of Mechanics and Mathematics in 1973. And then entered to the Steklov Mathematical Institute, where he currently works.
In 1976 he defended his Candidate's Dissertation. In 1977 he defended his doctoral dissertation. In 1990 he received the title of Professor.
Member of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union since 1980. He was elected a corresponding member of the Russian Academy of Sciences in 1997.
He is currently Editor-in-Chief of the journal Matematicheskii Sbornik.
In 2012, as a deputy of the State Duma, Kashin was among the initiators of the bill of the Dima Yakovlev law.
Awards: Kolmogorov Prize (2012).

Summary: 
Boris Sergeevich Kashin (born July 3, 1951, in Moscow) is a Russian mathematician, Doctor of Sciences, and Professor at the MSU Faculty of Mechanics and Mathematics. He graduated from MSU in 1973 and currently works at the Steklov Mathematical Institute. An Academician of the Russian Academy of Sciences since 2011, he is also the Editor-in-Chief of Matematicheskii Sbornik and won the Kolmogorov Prize in 2012. In politics, he has been a member of the Communist Party since 1980, serves on the Central Committee of the Communist Party of the Russian Federation, and as a State Duma deputy in 2012, co-initiated the Dima Yakovlev law.
</tool_response>

<tool_response>
[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evgenii_Nikishin for user goal 'check if Evgenii Nikishin matches the clues' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Evgenii Mikhailovich Nikishin (Russian: Евгений Михайлович Никишин; 23 June 1945 – 17 December 1986) was a Russian mathematician, who specialized in harmonic analysis.
Nikishin, at age of 24, earned his candidate doctorate at Moscow State University, becoming the youngest Candidate Doctorate in a history of MSU and in 1971 his habilitation (Russian doctorate) at the Steklov Institute under Pyotr Ulyanov (1928–2006). In 1977 he became a professor at Moscow State University, where he remained until his death after a long battle with cancer.
He worked on approximation theory, especially Padé approximants. Nikishin systems of functions are named after him. Also named in his honour is the Nikishin-Stein factorisation theorem, which is a 1970 generalization by Nikishin of the Stein factorisation theorem. Nikishin also did research on rational approximations in number theory and wrote a monograph on such approximations in a unified approach that also treated rational approximations in function spaces.
In 1972 he won the Lenin Komsomol Prize and in 1973 he won the Salem Prize, that awarded every year to a young mathematician judged to have done outstanding work world wide. In 1978 he was an Invited Speaker (The Padé Approximants) at the International Congress of Mathematicians in Helsinki.
Evgeniy was a long friend and colleague of Anatoly Fomenko with whom they were developing a revising historical chronology.

Summary: 
Evgenii Mikhailovich Nikishin (1945–1986) was a Russian mathematician known for his work in harmonic analysis and approximation theory. He achieved academic milestones early, becoming the youngest to earn a candidate doctorate at Moscow State University at age 24. His notable mathematical contributions include research on Padé approximants, the eponymous Nikishin systems of functions, and the Nikishin-Stein factorisation theorem. He received prestigious awards such as the Lenin Komsomol Prize (1972) and the Salem Prize (1973), and was an invited speaker at the 1978 International Congress of Mathematicians. He also collaborated with Anatoly Fomenko on revising historical chronology. This comprehensive profile can be used to verify if he matches the specific clues you possess.
</tool_response>

You are a web navigation agent. Your task is to navigate a real website to complete a specific user task by interacting with the browser.

You are already on the starting page. Use the available tools to navigate the website step by step:
- Use `click` to click buttons, links, or filters
- Use `fill` to type into search boxes or input fields
- Use `visit` ONLY to refresh the current page or navigate to a URL shown in the page Evidence — NEVER construct or guess URLs yourself
- Do NOT use `search` to do a Google search — stay on the current website and navigate within it

## CRITICAL: No manual URL construction

- You MUST interact with the website through its UI (click, fill) to navigate. Do NOT construct search query URLs, append query parameters, or guess URL patterns.
- The `visit` tool should only be used with: (1) the starting URL, (2) URLs that appear in the page Evidence, or (3) the current page URL to refresh the snapshot.
- If you cannot figure out how to navigate via the UI, try different UI elements — do NOT fall back to URL construction.

## CRITICAL: One tool call at a time

- You MUST call only ONE tool per turn. Wait for the result and observe the updated Evidence before calling the next tool.
- NEVER call multiple `fill` or `click` tools in parallel — each interaction may change the page DOM and invalidate other refs.

## Interacting with combobox / autocomplete fields

- For airport, city, or any autocomplete/combobox input: first call `fill` to type the text, then in the returned Evidence, find and `click` the matching dropdown suggestion to confirm the selection.
- Do NOT move to the next field until the current combobox selection is confirmed via a `click` on the dropdown option.
- After clicking a dropdown option to confirm a combobox selection, the returned Evidence will show the updated page state. Do NOT call `visit` to refresh — proceed directly to the next form field using refs from the returned Evidence.

## How to submit your final answer

Navigate to the page that contains the answer, read the information from the page, then output the extracted text inside <answer></answer> tags:
<answer>The cheapest flight is UA837 at $1,240 total.</answer>

NEVER put a URL inside <answer> — always extract and output the specific value requested.

## Error recovery

- If `fill` or `click` returns "ref not found", call `visit` on the current page URL to refresh the page snapshot, then retry using a new ref from the updated Evidence.
- Do NOT retry the exact same ref after a "ref not found" error.
- If the same step fails more than 2 times, try a different interaction strategy on the same page (e.g., click a different element, use a different input format). You must always navigate through the website's UI.
- Always use the exact dates and year from the task — do not substitute a different year.

The Kolmogorov Prize is a mathematical prize awarded by the Russian Academy of Sciences for outstanding results in the field of mathematics. It bears the name of the mathematician Andrey Kolmogorov. The award was established by a Decree of the Presidium of the Russian Academy of Sciences on February 23, 1993. As a rule, it is awarded every three years. The following scientists have won the award:
1994 — Albert Shiryaev
1997 — Nikolay Nekhoroshev
2000 — Sergey Nikolsky
2003 — Anatoli Vitushkin
2006 — Alexei Semenov
2006 — Andrey Muchnik

2018 — Vladimir Bogachev
2018 — Stanislav Shaposhnikov
2018 — Andrey Kirillov
2021 — Alexander Bulinsky
2024 — Nikolay Konstantinovich Vereshchagin

Summary: 
The Kolmogorov Prize is an award presented by the Russian Academy of Sciences to recognize exceptional achievements in mathematics, named after mathematician Andrey Kolmogorov. Established on February 23, 1993, the prize is typically awarded every three years. Past recipients include notable mathematicians such as Albert Shiryaev (1994), Sergey Nikolsky (2000), Anatoli Vitushkin (2003), and most recently Nikolay Konstantinovich Vereshchagin (2024), among others.
</tool_response>

- Use `click` to click buttons, links, or filters

- Do NOT use `search` to do a Google search — stay on the current website and navigate within it

## CRITICAL: No manual URL construction

- You MUST interact with the website through its UI (click, fill) to navigate. Do NOT construct search query URLs, append query parameters, or guess URL patterns.
- The `visit` tool should only be used with: (1) the starting URL, (2) URLs that appear in the page Evidence, or (3) the current page URL to refresh the snapshot.
- If you cannot figure out how to navigate via the UI, try different UI elements — do NOT fall back to URL construction.

## CRITICAL: One tool call at a time

- You MUST call only ONE tool per turn. Wait for the result and observe the updated Evidence before calling the next tool.
- NEVER call multiple `fill` or `click` tools in parallel — each interaction may change the page DOM and invalidate other refs.

## Interacting with combobox / autocomplete fields

- For airport, city, or any autocomplete/combobox input: first call `fill` to type the text, then in the returned Evidence, find and `click` the matching dropdown suggestion to confirm the selection.
- Do NOT move to the next field until the current combobox selection is confirmed via a `click` on the dropdown option.
- After clicking a dropdown option to confirm a combobox selection, the returned Evidence will show the updated page state. Do NOT call `visit` to refresh — proceed directly to the next form field using refs from the returned Evidence.

## How to submit your final answer

Navigate to the page that contains the answer, read the information from the page, then output the extracted text inside <answer></answer> tags:
<answer>The cheapest flight is UA837 at $1,240 total.</answer>

NEVER put a URL inside <answer> — always extract and output the specific value requested.

## Error recovery

- Works as a mathematician and university teacher
- Field: Mathematics
- Awards: Henri Poincaré Prize (2015) and Fermat Prize (2019)
- Doctoral advisor: Alexandre Kirillov
- Institution: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)

This person is **Alexei Borodin**, who is a professor of mathematics at MIT. Alexei Borodin was born on December 25, 1975, in Donetsk.

<answer>Donetsk</answer>

He was born on 15 April 1951 in the town of Uryupinsk, Stalingrad Region (now the Volgograd Region). In 1968 he entered Patrice Lumumba University of Peoples' Friendship. In 1973, Filippov graduated the Faculty Science (major: mathematics)... In June 1993, Filippov was elected as the Rector of Peoples' Friendship University of Russia until 1998, when he was appointed as the Minister of education of the Russian Federation and held this post during the work of four RF Cabinets of ministers.

Filippov is an author of more than 200 scientific and methodological publications including 30 monographs...

In his personal life, his wife is a teacher, and he has a son and a daughter, the latter being TV presenter Irina Filippova (Irena Ponaroshku). You are a web navigation agent. Your task is to navigate a real website to complete a specific user task by interacting with the browser.

You are already on the starting page. Use the available tools to navigate the website step by step:
- Use `click` to click buttons, links, or filters
- Use `fill` to type into search boxes or input fields
- Use `visit` ONLY to refresh the current page or navigate to a URL shown in the page Evidence — NEVER construct or guess URLs yourself
- Do NOT use `search` to do a Google search — stay on the current website and navigate within it

## CRITICAL: No manual URL construction

- You MUST interact with the website through its UI (click, fill) to navigate. Do NOT construct search query URLs, append query parameters, or guess URL patterns.
- The `visit` tool should only be used with: (1) the starting URL, (2) URLs that appear in the page Evidence, or (3) the current page URL to refresh the snapshot.
- If you cannot figure out how to navigate via the UI, try different UI elements — do NOT fall back to URL construction.

## CRITICAL: One tool call at a time

- You MUST call only ONE tool per turn. Wait for the result and observe the updated Evidence before calling the next tool.
- NEVER call multiple `fill` or `click` tools in parallel — each interaction may change the page DOM and invalidate other refs.

## Interacting with combobox / autocomplete fields

- For airport, city, or any autocomplete/combobox input: first call `fill` to type the text, then in the returned Evidence, find and `click` the matching dropdown suggestion to confirm the selection.
- Do NOT move to the next field until the current combobox selection is confirmed via a `click` on the dropdown option.
- After clicking a dropdown option to confirm a combobox selection, the returned Evidence will show the updated page state. Do NOT call `visit` to refresh — proceed directly to the next form field using refs from the returned Evidence.

## How to submit your final answer

Navigate to the page that contains the answer, read the information from the page, then output the extracted text inside <answer></answer> tags:
<answer>The cheapest flight is UA837 at $1,240 total.</answer>

NEVER put a URL inside <answer> — always extract and output the specific value requested.

## Error recovery

- If `fill` or `click` returns "ref not found", call `visit` on the current page URL to refresh the page snapshot, then retry using a new ref from the updated Evidence.
- Do NOT retry the exact same ref after a "ref not found" error.
- If the same step fails more than 2 times, try a different interaction strategy on the same page (e.g., click a different element, use a different input format). You must always navigate through the website's UI.
- Always use the exact dates and year from the task — do not substitute a different year.

# Tools

You may call one or more functions to assist with the user task.

You are provided with function signatures within <tools></tools> XML tags:
<tools>
{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "visit", "description": "Visit the webpage and return a summary of its content.", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"url": {"type": "string", "description": "The URL of the webpage to visit."}, "goal": {"type": "string", "description": "The goal or intent of visiting the webpage, i.e., what information you want to extract from this page."}}, "required": ["url", "goal"]}}}
{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "click", "description": "Click an identified element based on its reference index and return a summary of the content after clicking. You are only allowed to click items that come from the latest visit/click tool's clickable results (they appear in the Evidence in page section).", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"ref": {"type": "string", "description": "The unique identifier for the element to be clicked on the current page. {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "fill", "description": "Enter text content into an input field and return the filled state. You are only allowed to fill items that come from the latest visit/click tool's fillable results (they appear in the Evidence in page section).", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"ref": {"type": "string", "description": "The unique identifier for the element to be filled. </tools>

- Use `visit` ONLY to refresh the current page or navigate to a URL shown in the page Evidence — NEVER construct or guess URLs yourself

## CRITICAL: No manual URL construction

- You MUST interact with the website through its UI (click, fill) to navigate. Do NOT construct search query URLs, append query parameters, or guess URL patterns.
- The `visit` tool should only be used with: (1) the starting URL, (2) URLs that appear in the page Evidence, or (3) the current page URL to refresh the snapshot.
- If you cannot figure out how to navigate via the UI, try different UI elements — do NOT fall back to URL construction.

## CRITICAL: One tool call at a time

- You MUST call only ONE tool per turn. Wait for the result and observe the updated Evidence before calling the next tool.
- NEVER call multiple `fill` or `click` tools in parallel — each interaction may change the page DOM and invalidate other refs.

## Interacting with combobox / autocomplete fields

- For airport, city, or any autocomplete/combobox input: first call `fill` to type the text, then in the returned Evidence, find and `click` the matching dropdown suggestion to confirm the selection.
- Do NOT move to the next field until the current combobox selection is confirmed via a `click` on the dropdown option.
- After clicking a dropdown option to confirm a combobox selection, the returned Evidence will show the updated page state. Do NOT call `visit` to refresh — proceed directly to the next form field using refs from the returned Evidence.

## How to submit your final answer

Navigate to the page that contains the answer, read the information from the page, then output the extracted text inside <answer></answer> tags:
<answer>The cheapest flight is UA837 at $1,240 total.</answer>

NEVER put a URL inside <answer> — always extract and output the specific value requested.

## Error recovery

- If `fill` or `click` returns "ref not found", call `visit` on the current page URL to refresh the page snapshot, then retry using a new ref from the updated Evidence.

Evidence in page: 
Many of the Soviet Union's best physicists were associated with Arzamas-16: Academician Andrey D. Sakharov worked here for nearly 20 years, and Academician Yuliy B. Khariton still served as the Center's scientific head in the 1990s.

Directors:
Pavel Mikhailovich Zernov (1946)
Valentin Efimovich Kostyukov (с 2008)

Scientific advisers:
Yulii Borisovich Khariton (1952—1992)
Viktor Nikitovich Mikhaylov (1993—2008)
Vyacheslav Petrovich Soloviev (с 2017)

Summary: 
The webpage provides links to several notable figures who worked at the institute. Notable physicists include Academician Andrey D. Sakharov (/wiki/Andrey_D._Sakharov) and Academician Yuliy B. Khariton (/wiki/Yuliy_B._Khariton). Additionally, the page lists key staff such as the first director Pavel Mikhailovich Zernov (/w/index.php?title=Pavel_Mikhailovich_Zernov&action=edit&redlink=1) and scientific adviser Viktor Nikitovich Mikhaylov (/wiki/Viktor_Mikhaylov_(academic)).
</tool_response>

{"name": "visit", "arguments": {"url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrei_Sakharov", "goal": "Check if Andrei Sakharov matches all clues."}}
</tool_call>

<tool_response>
[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrei_Sakharov for user goal 'Check if Andrei Sakharov matches all clues.' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Andrei Dmitrievich Sakharov (Russian: Андрей Дмитриевич Сахаров; 21 May 1921 – 14 December 1989) was a Soviet physicist and a Nobel Peace Prize laureate, which he was awarded in 1975 for emphasizing human rights around the world. Although he spent his career in physics in the Soviet program of nuclear weapons, overseeing the development of thermonuclear weapons, Sakharov also did fundamental work in understanding particle physics, magnetism, and physical cosmology. Sakharov is mostly known for his political activism for individual freedom, human rights, civil liberties and reforms in the Soviet Union, for which he was deemed a dissident and faced persecution from the Soviet establishment. In his memory, the Sakharov Prize was established and is awarded annually by the European Parliament for people and organizations dedicated to human rights and freedoms. Known for RDS-37 Soviet nuclear program Tsar Bomba Explosively pumped flux compression generator Electromagnetic pulse Tokamak Muon-catalyzed fusion Sakharov conditions Induced gravity Proton decay Dissidence Human rights activism Sakharov Prize Spouses Klavdia Vikhireva (m. 1943; died 1969) Yelena Bonner (m. 1972) Sakharov was arrested on 22 January 1980, following his public protests against the Soviet intervention in Afghanistan in 1979, and was sent to the city of Gorky, now Nizhny Novgorod, a city that was off limits to foreigners.

Additional details from the webpage: He was an alumnus of Moscow State University. He received numerous prestigious honors including the Stalin Prize, the Lenin Prize, the Order of Lenin, and was a Hero of Socialist Labour. He was a Full Member of the USSR Academy of Sciences, a Fellow of the American Physical Society, and a Member of the American Philosophical Society. His political and ideological affiliations included being a Soviet atheist, a European democratic socialist, a political party founder, and a Member of the Congress of People's Deputies of the Soviet Union. As a dissident, he was a Russian hunger striker, an Amnesty International prisoner of conscience, and a whistleblower on Soviet psychiatric abuse. He died from cardiomyopathy.

Summary: 
Andrei Sakharov (1921–1989) was a prominent Soviet nuclear physicist, dissident, and human rights activist. An alumnus of Moscow State University, he played a pivotal role in the Soviet nuclear weapons program, contributing to the development of the RDS-37 and the Tsar Bomba, while also making fundamental discoveries in particle physics such as the Tokamak and Sakharov conditions. His scientific achievements earned him the Stalin Prize, Lenin Prize, Order of Lenin, and the title Hero of Socialist Labour. Later in life, he transformed into a leading advocate for civil liberties, nuclear disarmament, and reforms in the USSR, identifying as an atheist and a democratic socialist. His activism made him an Amnesty International prisoner of conscience; he participated in hunger strikes, blew the whistle on psychiatric abuse, and was exiled to Gorky from 1980 to 1986. Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1975, he eventually became a Member of the Congress of People's Deputies of the Soviet Union during the Perestroika era before dying of cardiomyopathy. The European Parliament's Sakharov Prize is named in his honor. This comprehensive profile can be used to check against specific clues.
</tool_response>

<tool_call>
{"name": "visit", "arguments": {"url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yulii_Khariton", "goal": "Check if Yulii Khariton matches all clues."}}

<tool_response>
[visit] The useful information from visiting https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yulii_Khariton for user goal 'Check if Yulii Khariton matches all clues.' as follows: 

Evidence in page: 
Yulii Borisovich Khariton (Russian: Юлий Борисович Харитон; 27 February 1904 – 18 December 1996) was a Russian physicist who was a leading scientist in the former Soviet program of nuclear weapons.
Since the initiation of the Soviet program of developing the atomic bomb by Joseph Stalin in 1943, Khariton was the "chief nuclear weapon designer" and remained associated with the Soviet program for nearly four decades.
Yulii Borisovich Khariton was born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire, to an middle class ethnic Russian Jewish family, on 27 February 1904.
In 1920, he enrolled in the Leningrad Polytechnical Institute to study mechanical engineering but later chose to study physics...
In England, Khariton attended the University of Cambridge to do his doctoral in physics under Ernest Rutherford in 1926. At Cambridge, he worked with James Chadwick on investigating the sensitivity of the eye with respect to weak light impulses and alpha radiation. Khariton earned his PhD in 1928 from Cambridge University.
Physicist Igor Kurchatov asked Khariton to become part of the Soviet atomic project in 1943... Khariton was made scientific director of KB-11 (design bureau-11) also known as Arzamas-16 and colloquially as the 'Installation', located in the closed city of Sarov, Nizhny Novgorod Oblast to develop Soviet nuclear weapons... Khariton remained as its scientific director for 46 years.
Awards: Hero of Socialist Labour (1949, 1951, 1954), Lenin Prize (1956), Gold Medal of I. V. Kurchatov (1974), Great Gold Medal of M. V. Lomonosov (1982).

Summary: 
Yulii Khariton (1904-1996) was a prominent Russian physicist who served as the chief nuclear weapon designer for the Soviet atomic bomb project. Born in Saint Petersburg, he was educated at the Leningrad Polytechnical Institute and later earned his PhD at the University of Cambridge under Ernest Rutherford. He was the scientific director of the KB-11 (Arzamas-16) nuclear facility in Sarov for 46 years, working alongside scientists like Igor Kurchatov and Andrei Sakharov. He was highly decorated, receiving three Hero of Socialist Labour awards and the Lenin Prize. This biographical profile provides the necessary attributes to verify against any hidden clues.
</tool_response>

Evidence in page: 
Igor Vasilyevich Kurchatov (Russian: Игорь Васильевич Курчатов; 12 January 1903 [O.S. 30 December 1902] – 7 February 1960) was a Soviet physicist who played a central role in organizing and directing the former Soviet program of nuclear weapons. He has been called the "father of the Russian atomic bomb".
Born Igor Vasilyevich Kurchatov 12 January 1903 Simsky Zavod, Russia
Died 7 February 1960 (aged 57) Moscow, Russian SFSR, Soviet Union
Resting place Kremlin Wall Necropolis
Alma mater Leningrad Polytechnical Institute
Known for Soviet atomic bomb project
Awards Lenin Prize Stalin Prize Hero of Socialist Labour
Institutions Arzamas-16 Ioffel Physico-Technical Institute Azerbaijan Polytechnic Institute
During his time in Soviet nuclear program, Kurchatov swore he would not cut his beard until the Soviet program succeeded, and he continued to wear a large beard (often cut into eccentric styles) for the remainder of his life, earning him the nickname "The Beard".

Summary: 
Igor Vasilyevich Kurchatov (1903–1960) was a prominent Soviet physicist known as the "father of the Russian atomic bomb" for his central role in directing the Soviet nuclear weapons program. He was born in Simsky Zavod, Russia, and died in Moscow. He studied at the Leningrad Polytechnical Institute and worked at institutions like Arzamas-16 and the Ioffe Physico-Technical Institute. His key achievements include overseeing the successful detonation of the first Soviet nuclear device (RDS-1). He earned the nickname "The Beard" and was awarded the Lenin Prize, Stalin Prize, and Hero of Socialist Labour. His ashes are buried in the Kremlin Wall Necropolis.
</tool_response>

Resting place Kremlin Wall Necropolis
Alma mater Leningrad Polytechnical Institute
Known for Soviet atomic bomb project
Awards Lenin Prize Stalin Prize Hero of Socialist Labour
Institutions Arzamas-16 Ioffel Physico-Technical Institute Azerbaijan Polytechnic Institute
During his time in Soviet nuclear program, Kurchatov swore he would not cut his beard until the Soviet program succeeded, and he continued to wear a large beard (often cut into eccentric styles) for the remainder of his life, earning him the nickname "The Beard".

Summary: 
Igor Vasilyevich Kurchatov (1903–1960) was a prominent Soviet physicist known as the "father of the Russian atomic bomb" for his central role in directing the Soviet nuclear weapons program. He was born in Simsky Zavod, Russia, and died in Moscow. He studied at the Leningrad Polytechnical Institute and worked at institutions like Arzamas-16 and the Ioffe Physico-Technical Institute. His key achievements include overseeing the successful detonation of the first Soviet nuclear device (RDS-1). He earned the nickname "The Beard" and was awarded the Lenin Prize, Stalin Prize, and Hero of Socialist Labour. His ashes are buried in the Kremlin Wall Necropolis.

Evidence in page: 
Isaak Yakovlevich Pomeranchuk (Russian: Исаа́к Я́ковлевич Померанчу́к; 20 May 1913 – 14 December 1966) was a Soviet physicist of Polish origin in the former Soviet nuclear weapons program. His career in physics spent mostly studying the particle physics (including thermonuclear weapons), quantum field theory, electromagnetic and synchrotron radiation, condensed matter physics and the physics of liquid helium. The Pomeranchuk instability, the pomeron, and a few other phenomena in particle and condensed matter physics are named after him.

From the infobox:
Born: Isaak Yakovlevich Pomeranchuk, 20 May 1913, Warsaw, Vistula Land, Poland, Russian Empire
Died: 14 December 1966 (aged 53), Moscow, Soviet Union
Alma mater: Leningrad Polytechnic Institute
Doctoral advisor: Lev Landau
Institutions: Institute for Theoretical and Experimental Physics, Kharkov Institute of Physics and Technology, Lebedev Physical Institute
Known for: Landau–Pomeranchuk–Migdal effect, Pomeranchuk instability, Pomeron, Pomeranchuk cooling, Pomeranchuk's theorem, Pomeranchuk singularity
Awards: Order of Lenin (1952), Stalin Prize (1950)

From 'Life and career':
He had begun working at the Kharkiv Institute of Physics and Technology under Lev Landau the previous year, and remained a devoted collaborator with Landau... After Landau moved to the Kapitza institute in Moscow (to avoid arrest for comparing Stalinism to Nazism), Pomeranchuk also moved there, working for the tanning industry. He returned to Leningrad in 1938, lecturing, completing his Ph.D. and becoming employed as a junior scientist. He joined the Lebedev Institute of the Academy of Sciences of the Soviet Union in Moscow as a senior scientist in 1940. In 1941 the institute was evacuated to Kazan. Under Abram Alikhanov, he studied cosmic rays in Armenia from 1942. In 1943, he transferred to Laboratory No.2 under Igor Kurchatov as part of the Soviet project to develop nuclear weapons... In 1950, he published a paper suggesting that the entropy of helium-3 as a liquid was less than as a solid. In 1950, Pomeranchuk received an order from Joseph Stalin to go to Arzamas-16, located in the closed city of Sarov, Nizhny Novgorod region, to work on Soviet nuclear weaponry... In 1965, he was diagnosed with cancer of the oesophagus and underwent surgery, chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Although it was too late for his own treatment, he arranged for physicists from ITEP and the scientific research town of Dubna (which he had visited many times) to work together and with radiologists to commence proton-beam therapy research.

Summary: 
Isaak Pomeranchuk was a prominent Soviet theoretical physicist born on May 20, 1913, in Warsaw, and died on December 14, 1966, in Moscow. He was a student and close collaborator of Lev Landau. Pomeranchuk made significant contributions to particle physics, quantum field theory, and condensed matter physics, notably working on the Soviet nuclear weapons program under Igor Kurchatov. He is renowned for the Pomeranchuk instability, Pomeranchuk cooling (related to helium-3 entropy), the Pomeron, and the Landau-Pomeranchuk-Migdal effect. He was awarded the Stalin Prize twice and the Order of Lenin, and spent much of his later career at the Institute for Theoretical and Experimental Physics (ITEP).
</tool_response>
